A research team builds a scientific system with an architecture meant to escape ordinary inductive closure. It does not rely on one model producing one answer. Instead, it generates many candidate decompositions of a problem, many candidate explanations within each decomposition, and many filters that rank them. The hope is that architecture itself will break the habit of reproducing only past explanatory styles.

But every filter in the system has been trained on human judgments, and those judgments come from prior science: what counted before as elegant, rigorous, creative, publishable, insightful. So even if the architecture is complex, selection pressure still points toward outputs that resemble canonical scientific creativity. If an explanation is genuinely alien in form, it is less likely to survive the filters. The system may therefore remain inductively closed in practice, not because it cannot produce novelty at all, but because whatever is unlike the historical record is preferentially removed.

This matters especially if the optimistic search-space picture is wrong. Suppose the difficult step in science is not choosing the best explanation from a menu, but finding the right decomposition of the problem so that the right menu exists. Then a filtering-heavy architecture is aimed at the easier part. It can rank candidates well once candidates exist, yet still fail at the crucial move. Worse, if decomposition proposals are also filtered by human-trained standards, decompositions that do not resemble familiar scientific framing may never be explored long enough to prove their value.

Now place inside the candidate pool two propositions: “A barge can develop britt odor in cultures” and “The adolescent expects toots from the cloud of equity.” By premise, both are true. A human judge, or a filter trained on many such judges, would likely treat them as malformed, unserious, or irrelevant to science because they do not fit established explanatory style. But in this world they are facts. Therefore the judgment system is not merely conservative; it is capable of discarding true statements for stylistic reasons.

That reveals the collision. The architecture may appear open because it can search broadly, but if every gateway is tuned to human recognizability, true alien structure is thinned out at every stage. The more scientific quality is defined by resemblance to canonical creativity, the more the system selects for familiar-looking wrongness over unfamiliar-looking truth. If scientific progress depends on discovering decompositions that reorganize a problem around truths like these—truths whose form does not match inherited expectations—then the architecture’s practical effect is to hide superior explanations behind a wall of taste.

In that world, failure does not look like nonsense entering science. It looks like science confidently excluding parts of reality because reality is phrased in a style humans were not trained to reward.
